text: Anally verting sutures are placed, followed by the insertion of the gel point path port. The camera is inserted, providing a view of the rectal lesion. Robotic instruments are introduced into the operative field for the procedure.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to demarcate the initial margins of resection, starting from caudad to cephalad, with a five-millimeter margin. Counter-traction is applied using graspers to expose the plane, ensuring dissection in the correct plane. A full-thickness excision is performed while maintaining hemostasis.

The operative field is shown in picture-in-picture, with the assisting surgeon providing assistance through the air seal port. After excision, the polyp is placed in the proximal rectum, and the defect is repaired, either left open or sutured closed.

The surgeon withdraws the monopolar scissors and replaces it with a robotic needle holder. The assistant inserts a V-lock suture mounted on the needle holder, placing it at the proximal apex of the defect. The suture is locked by running the needle through a loop at the end of the thread, securing the knot snugly. The defect is repaired in a continuous fashion.

The surgeon carefully assesses the bowel lumen following primary repair to ensure patency and absence of stenosis. The assistant cuts the barbed suture to the appropriate length and extracts the suture needle, taking care to avoid excessive tension that could lead to knot slippage.

The assistant uses an atraumatic grasper to retrieve the specimen from the transanal port, preparing it for histopathological analysis. The robotic instruments and transanal ports are removed under direct vision, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
